questions.question: Sepsis is frequently associated with which two findings?
options: A. Rash and joint pain
B. Fever and altered level of consciousness
C. Hypoglycemia and bradycardia
D. Cough and sore throat only
questions.rationale: Fever and altered LOC are the classic sepsis association, prompting an active search for an infectious source.

questions.question: What is a common cause of sepsis in the elderly?
options: A. Simple viral cold
B. Pneumonia or a UTI secondary to an indwelling urinary catheter
C. Migraine headache
D. Seasonal allergies
questions.rationale: Pneumonia and catheter-associated urinary tract infections are common sepsis sources in elderly patients.

questions.question: Which findings are MOST diagnostic for anaphylaxis?
options: A. Mild rash only
B. Hypotension and airway swelling (stridor, angioedema)
C. Fever and cough
D. Isolated abdominal pain
questions.rationale: Hypotension combined with airway swelling (stridor, angioedema) are the most diagnostic findings for anaphylaxis, reflecting the cardiovascular and respiratory system involvement.

questions.question: What is first-line treatment for anaphylaxis?
options: A. Oral antihistamine
B. IM epinephrine
C. IV corticosteroids
D. Nebulized saline
questions.rationale: IM epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis; antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts, not substitutes.

questions.question: Why are antihistamines and corticosteroids NOT considered first-line for anaphylaxis?
options: A. They work faster than epinephrine
B. They have a slower onset of action and do not reverse the acute hypotension and airway compromise
C. They are more effective than epinephrine
D. They are contraindicated in all allergic reactions
questions.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids act more slowly and do not address the acute life threats (hypotension, airway compromise) the way epinephrine does — they support but do not replace it.

questions.question: An elderly nursing home resident presents with a fall and new confusion but no fever. What should still be considered?
options: A. Sepsis can be ruled out since there is no fever
B. Sepsis remains a possibility — elderly patients may not mount a classic fever response and can present atypically
C. This is always simple dementia progression
D. No further workup is needed
questions.rationale: Elderly patients can present with vague findings like falls or confusion without a robust fever response, so sepsis should remain on the differential despite the absent fever.

questions.question: What underlying physiologic process do sepsis and anaphylaxis share?
options: A. Cardiogenic pump failure
B. Distributive shock from pathologic vasodilation
C. Obstructive shock from mechanical compression
D. They share no common physiology
questions.rationale: Both sepsis and anaphylaxis cause distributive shock through pathologic vasodilation that drops systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure.

questions.question: A patient develops hives, stridor, and a blood pressure of 78/40 after a bee sting. What is the correct FIRST treatment?
options: A. Diphenhydramine (antihistamine) only
B. IM epinephrine
C. Oral corticosteroids
D. Wait and observe before treating
questions.rationale: With hypotension and airway swelling (stridor) after a known allergen exposure, this is anaphylaxis — IM epinephrine is the first-line, life-saving treatment, given without delay.

questions.question: Which body systems can be involved in anaphylaxis?
options: A. Only the skin
B. Skin, respiratory, cardiovascular, and gastrointestinal systems
C. Only the cardiovascular system
D. Only the respiratory system
questions.rationale: Anaphylaxis is a systemic reaction affecting skin (hives), respiratory (wheeze/stridor/angioedema), cardiovascular (hypotension/tachycardia), and GI (nausea/vomiting) systems.

questions.question: Where should you actively search for a sepsis source in an elderly patient with fever and confusion?
options: A. Only the skin
B. Lungs, urinary tract (especially with an indwelling catheter), skin/wounds, and abdomen
C. Only the abdomen
D. No source search is needed once sepsis is suspected
questions.rationale: A deliberate search across likely sources (respiratory, urinary, skin/wound, abdominal) is part of sepsis assessment, especially in elderly patients with device-related infection risk.

questions.question: Why is delaying epinephrine in favor of an antihistamine considered a dangerous error in anaphylaxis?
options: A. It is not dangerous — antihistamines work just as fast
B. Anaphylaxis can progress to complete airway obstruction or cardiovascular collapse within minutes, and only epinephrine reverses this acutely
C. Antihistamines are contraindicated entirely in anaphylaxis
D. Epinephrine has no advantage over antihistamines
questions.rationale: Because anaphylaxis can rapidly progress to life-threatening airway or cardiovascular compromise, and epinephrine is the only agent that acutely reverses this, delaying it for a slower-acting antihistamine risks patient deterioration.

questions.question: What connects the management of sepsis and anaphylaxis to the broader shock chapter?
options: A. Nothing connects them
B. Both are distributive shock, sharing principles like keeping the patient warm and fluid resuscitation while treating the underlying cause
C. Both require identical drug treatment
D. Only sepsis is a form of shock
questions.rationale: Recognizing sepsis and anaphylaxis as distributive shock connects their management to shared shock principles (warming, fluids) while still requiring cause-specific treatment (epinephrine vs. source control/antibiotics).

questions.question: A patient with an indwelling urinary catheter develops fever and new lethargy. What should be on your differential?
options: A. Sepsis from a catheter-associated urinary tract infection
B. Simple dehydration only, unrelated to the catheter
C. Anaphylaxis from the catheter material
D. No concern since a catheter cannot cause infection
questions.rationale: Indwelling urinary catheters are a recognized common source of sepsis in patients with fever and altered mental status, particularly in elderly populations.

questions.question: Which route is used for the first-line epinephrine dose in anaphylaxis?
options: A. Oral
B. Intramuscular (IM)
C. Topical
D. Subcutaneous only, never IM
questions.rationale: IM epinephrine is the first-line route for anaphylaxis treatment, providing rapid absorption to reverse hypotension and airway compromise.
